draft.introduction: Jerome’s Letter 22, addressed to the virgin Eustochium, is his most famous and personal letter, advocating ascetic virginity. It contains the famous dream in which Christ rebukes him: “You are a Ciceronian, not a Christian.”

translation.text: I was seized and dragged before the Judge. When asked my condition, I replied that I was a Christian. But he who presided said: ‘You lie. You are a Ciceronian, not a Christian. For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.’ I was struck dumb, and amid the strokes of the whip I cried out: ‘Have mercy upon me, O Lord.’
translation.original_text: Interrogatus condicionem Christianum me esse respondi. Et ille qui residebat: Mentiris, ait, Ciceronianus es, non Christianus

translator_notes.note: Jerome’s dream crystallizes the tension between classical culture and Christian faith that haunted the entire patristic period. His vow to renounce pagan literature proved impossible to keep—his biblical scholarship remained deeply indebted to classical philology.
